title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction Yuan Zhou1, Qingshan Xu1, Jiequan Cui1, Junbao Zhou1, Jing Zhang2, Richang Hong3, Hanwang Zhang1 1Nanyang Technological University 2Beihang University 3Hefei University of Technology {yuan.zhou, qingshan.xu@ntu.edu.sg, hanwangzhang}@ntu.edu.sg, zhang_jing@buaa.edu.cn {bowmanchow, jiequancui, hongrc.hfut}@gmail.com Abstract Recently, large efforts have been made to design efficient linear-complexity visual Transformers. However, current linear attention models are generally unsuitable to be de- ployed in resource-constrained mobile devices, due to suf- fering from either few efficiency gains or significant accu- racy drops. In this paper, we propose a new deCoupled duAl-interactive lineaR attEntion (CARE) mechanism, re- vealing that features’ decoupling and interaction can fully unleash the power of linear attention. We first propose an asymmetrical feature decoupling strategy that asymmetri- cally decouples the learning process for local inductive bias and long-range dependencies, thereby preserving sufficient local and global information while effectively enhancing the efficiency of models. Then, a dynamic memory unit is em- ployed to maintain critical information along the network pipeline. Moreover, we design a dual interaction module to effectively facilitate interaction between local inductive bias and long-range information as well as among features at different layers. By adopting a decoupled learning way and fully exploiting complementarity across

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: memory unit is em- ployed to maintain critical information along the network pipeline. Moreover, we design a dual interaction module to effectively facilitate interaction between local inductive bias and long-range information as well as among features at different layers. By adopting a decoupled learning way and fully exploiting complementarity across features, our method can achieve both high efficiency and accuracy. Ex- tensive experiments on ImageNet-1K, COCO, and ADE20K datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, e.g., achieving 78.4/82.1% top-1 accuracy on ImagegNet-1K at the cost of only 0.7/1.9 GMACs. Codes will be released on https://github.com/zhouyuan888888/CARE-Transformer. 1. Introduction Transformers [45] were originally designed for natural lan- guage processing tasks due to their inherent superiority in global receptive field and parallelizability. In the past few years, they have been successfully introduced to computer vision, including image classification [12, 13, 29, 47], se- mantic segmentation [4, 39, 52, 65], object detection [2, 26], and image editing [63, 66]. Nevertheless, the quadratic CARE-S2 CARE-S1 CARE-S0 Figure 1. text: and image editing [63, 66]. Nevertheless, the quadratic CARE-S2 CARE-S1 CARE-S0 Figure 1. Visualized comparison of the balance between accuracy, latency, and GMACs for our CARE Transformers and recent typ- ical mobile-friendly models. In the figure, the larger the marker, the more GMACs the model consumes. “SA”, “LA”, and “GNN” indicate that the methods are based on Self-Attention, Linear At- tention, and Graph Neural Networks. For more details, please refer to Table 1. complexity of self-attention severely limits the applicabil- ity of Transformers in resource-constrained scenarios, such as on mobile devices. Recently, large efforts have been dedicated to design- ing efficient linear-complexity visual Transformers, which can be grouped into two main roadmaps: i) limiting the receptive field by using local attention; ii) utilizing linear attention to lower down complexity. Local attention im- proves the efficiency of visual Transformers at the cost of sacrificing their capability to capture long-range dependen- cies [12, 20, 29, 47, 51], which have been proven to play a critical role in learning high-quality feature representations [11, 30]. In contrast to local attention, linear attention can be seen as a more elegant way to solve the quadratic com- plexity issue [5, 24, 32, 38]. In fact, the

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: [12, 20, 29, 47, 51], which have been proven to play a critical role in learning high-quality feature representations [11, 30]. In contrast to local attention, linear attention can be seen as a more elegant way to solve the quadratic com- plexity issue [5, 24, 32, 38]. In fact, the quadratic complex- ity problem in self-attention primarily stems from the use of an explicit similarity measurement between queries and keys. Therefore, linear attention utilizes a kernel trick to re- arXiv:2411.16170v2 [cs.CV] 25 Jan 2026

Comparisons between (a) the stacked learning approach [17] and (b) our asymmetrical decoupled learning way (d1 < d2) that learns local inductive bias and long-range dependencies sep- arately and further alleviates the quadratic overhead of linear at- tention to channel dimension, i.e., O(hwd2 1). Local inductive bias is learned by using depth-wise convolutions [6, 12, 17] and long- range dependencies are captured by utilizing linear attention [17]. move the softmax function from self-attention and changes the overall computation order, therefore scaling computa- tion complexity to the linear while still ensuring the global receptive field of models. A main drawback of linear attention lies in its high en- tropy property, meaning it struggles to suppress the influ- ence of irrelevant tokens because of not explicitly measur- ing tokens’ relationships [60]. Thus, as shown in Figure 2 (a), the recent work [17] proposed to incorporate linear at- tention

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: A main drawback of linear attention lies in its high en- tropy property, meaning it struggles to suppress the influ- ence of irrelevant tokens because of not explicitly measur- ing tokens’ relationships [60]. Thus, as shown in Figure 2 (a), the recent work [17] proposed to incorporate linear at- tention with local inductive bias in a stacked manner, so as to guide models to be aware of global contexts but focus more on important neighboring pixels. The stacked local enhancement can boost the effectiveness of linear attention. But we found that its integration between local inductive bias and long-range information remains inflexible and in- efficient, which is realized through the alternate use of con- volutions and linear attention, making it still unsuitable to be deployed in resource-constrained scenarios where mod- els’ efficiency is critical. This naturally motives us to ask two questions: 7→Q1. Is stacking the optimal approach to fully leverage the advantages of local inductive bias and long-range information? 7→Q2. Is it possible to enhance linear visual Transformers’ efficiency and accuracy at the same time? Aiming to answer these two questions, we propose a new deCoupled duAl-interactive lineaR attEntion (CARE) mechanism and introduce our CARE Transformers. For the question

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: leverage the advantages of local inductive bias and long-range information? 7→Q2. Is it possible to enhance linear visual Transformers’ efficiency and accuracy at the same time? Aiming to answer these two questions, we propose a new deCoupled duAl-interactive lineaR attEntion (CARE) mechanism and introduce our CARE Transformers. For the question 7→Q1, we argue that asymmetrical feature decou- pling can fully unleash the power of linear attention. As il- lustrated in Figure 2 (b), by decoupling features in channel dimension, inputs do not need to undergo all the convolu- tion and linear attention operations. Besides, the asymmetry (i.e., d1 < d2) can further alleviate the quadratic overhead of linear attention to channel dimension (i.e., O(hwd2 1)). Based on the answer to 7→Q1, we argue that by adopting the asymmetrical decoupled learning way and fully consid- ering complementarity across features, the question 7→Q2 can be solved. To fully exploit the complementarity of fea- tures, we first design a dynamic memory unit to preserve the critical information along the network pipeline. Then, we introduce a dual interaction module to effectively facilitate interaction between local bias and long-range dependencies as well as among features at different layers. The asym- metrical decoupling strategy saves computational

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: fea- tures, we first design a dynamic memory unit to preserve the critical information along the network pipeline. Then, we introduce a dual interaction module to effectively facilitate interaction between local bias and long-range dependencies as well as among features at different layers. The asym- metrical decoupling strategy saves computational costs on learning local inductive bias and global information, while cross-feature interaction can flexibly and effectively exploit the information in learned features, thus helping our decou- pled learning approach achieve both high efficiency and ac- curacy. All-in-all, the contributions of this paper can be summa- rized below: • We propose an asymmetrical decoupling strategy that learns local inductive bias and long-range dependencies in a divide-and-conquer way, thereby preserving suffi- cient local and global cues while further improving mod- els’ efficiency. • We design a dynamic memory unit and a dual interaction module to fully leverage the complementarity between lo- cal inductive bias and long-range dependencies as well as among features at different layers, helping our decoupled learning approach to achieve both high efficiency and ac- curacy. • We conduct extensive experiments on ImageNet-1K, ADE20K, and COCO datasets to validate the effective- ness of our approach. The experimental results demon- strate

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: inductive bias and long-range dependencies as well as among features at different layers, helping our decoupled learning approach to achieve both high efficiency and ac- curacy. • We conduct extensive experiments on ImageNet-1K, ADE20K, and COCO datasets to validate the effective- ness of our approach. The experimental results demon- strate the competitive performance of our models, e.g., achieving 78.4/82.1% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K with 1.1/2.0ms latency on iPhone13 and 0.8/1.5ms la- tency on iPad Pro. 2. Related Work We briefly review the research that is relevant to our work in this section. We review local attention in Section 2.1 and in- troduce the recent advance in linear attention in Section 2.2. 2.1. Local Attention Swin [29] is a typical method based on local attention, where a shifted local attention was introduced to facilitate communication between features across windows. A halo- based local attention was proposed in HaloNet [46] to better capture information from the neighborhood of each query. NAT [19] proposed a neighborhood attention, which can be more dynamic and flexible to aggregate information from neighboring pixels, thereby overcoming the drawback of the non-overlapping window partition. To better capture

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: data-dependent local patterns, a deformable attention and a bi-level routing strategy were introduced by DAT [50] and BiFormer [67], in order to dynamically search important re- gions or pixes according to queries. CSWin [12] proposed a cross-shaped local attention, which further reduces com- putational expenses via calculating attention in horizontal and vertical stripes. Using local attention can alleviate the quadratic complexity issue of self-attention but inevitably restricts models’ capability to capture long-range informa- tion, which is critical for learning high-quality features. 2.2. Linear Attention Nystromformer [53] proposed to use the matrix approxima- tion strategy Nystrom to approximate self-attention. Per- former [5] designed a random orthogonal feature mecha- nism to provide an unbiased linear-complexity estimation for self-attention. SOFT [32] replaced the softmax function with Gaussian kernels, enabling self-attention to be approx- imated by low-rank matrix decomposition. EfficientAtt [38] proposed to apply softmax to queries and keys separately, therefore decomposing self-attention to the linear. [55] fur- ther reduced the head dimension of linear attention by in- troducing a head competition mechanism. A focused linear attention was proposed by FLatten [16] to further boost the focus ability and diversity of linear attention. SLAB [15] advanced [16] by introducing a re-parameterized Batch- Norm, thereby

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: the linear. [55] fur- ther reduced the head dimension of linear attention by in- troducing a head competition mechanism. A focused linear attention was proposed by FLatten [16] to further boost the focus ability and diversity of linear attention. SLAB [15] advanced [16] by introducing a re-parameterized Batch- Norm, thereby further improving the speed of linear atten- tion models. Agent [18] combined softmax and linear atten- tion together by introducing agent tokens. Directly approxi- mating self-attention by using linear complexity is difficult. So, recently, MLLA [17] enhanced linear attention by lever- aging complementarity between local and global informa- tion in a stacked manner. Our work takes a step further on MLLA [17] and reveals that features’ asymmetrical decou- pling and interaction can fully unleash the power of linear attention. 3. Methodology 3.1. Preliminary Self-Attention. In a self-attention, the features X ∈Rhw×d of tokens are first projected into three subspaces, Q = XWQ, K = XWK, and V = XWV , called queries, keys, and values respectively. Then, Equation (1) is utilized to calculate the outputs O ∈Rhw×d of the self-attention Oi = N X j=1 exp �QiK⊤ j  PN t=1 exp �QiK⊤ t VjWO, (1) where Qi, Ki, and

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: = XWQ, K = XWK, and V = XWV , called queries, keys, and values respectively. Then, Equation (1) is utilized to calculate the outputs O ∈Rhw×d of the self-attention Oi = N X j=1 exp �QiK⊤ j  PN t=1 exp �QiK⊤ t VjWO, (1) where Qi, Ki, and Vi represent the query, key, and value about the i-th token, and WO denotes the weights for output projection. Linear Attention. In linear attention, a kernel trick exp(QK⊤) = φ(Q)φ(K)⊤is further employed to change the computation order of Equation (1) and scale the com- plexity to the linear, Oi = N X j=1 φ(Qi)(φ(Kj)⊤Vj) φ(Qi) PN t=1 φ(Kt)⊤WO. (2) Recently, [40, 54] found setting the kernel function φ(·) as an identity operator can achieve satisfying performance. Thus, following these methods, we set φ(·) as an identity in our work. 3.2. Motivation Linear Attention with Stacked Local Enhancement. In order to leverage the advantages of both local and global information, a stacked local enhancement strategy was in- troduced in [17], which can be described by Equation (3): O = LocalBias(LinearAtt(LocalBias(X))), (3) where LinearAtt(·) represents a linear attention operation and LocalBias(·) denotes a local inductive bias learner. Lo- cal inductive bias guides

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: leverage the advantages of both local and global information, a stacked local enhancement strategy was in- troduced in [17], which can be described by Equation (3): O = LocalBias(LinearAtt(LocalBias(X))), (3) where LinearAtt(·) represents a linear attention operation and LocalBias(·) denotes a local inductive bias learner. Lo- cal inductive bias guides models to focus more on neigh- boring pixels, thus relieving the interference of irrelevant information in learned long-range dependencies. Each lo- cal inductive bias learner in [17] is implemented by using a depth-wise convolution. We denote the kernel size of these convolutions as k×k, and therefore the computational com- plexity cost in learning local inductive bias and long-range dependencies is Ω= 2k2hwd | {z } Local inductive bias + Projection z }| { 4hwd2 + QKV Multiplication z }| { 2hwd2 | {z } Long-range dependencies . (4) ⅁Proposition 1. We argue that the process of the local en- hancement can be explicitly divided into two steps: 7→S1. learning local inductive bias and long-range information from inputs; 7→S2. fully exploiting the complementarity be- tween them. However, [17] does not distinguish these two steps and implicitly unifies them into one pipeline, where the over- all process is formulated as the alternate

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: can be explicitly divided into two steps: 7→S1. learning local inductive bias and long-range information from inputs; 7→S2. fully exploiting the complementarity be- tween them. However, [17] does not distinguish these two steps and implicitly unifies them into one pipeline, where the over- all process is formulated as the alternate use of convolution and linear attention operations to aggregate local and global information. ⅁Proposition 2. Unifying these two steps leads to two drawbacks: 7→D1. input features should undergo all of the local and global processors, which is the computation bot- tleneck of the stacked local enhancement; 7→D2. the flex- ibility of algorithms is damaged severely, as this makes it hard to specifically design a more effective fusion block to facilitate information exchange between local and global features. The above observations motivate us to propose a new divide-and-conquer way to efficiently learn and exploit the information of local inductive bias and long-range depen- dencies.

Schematic illustration for CARE Transformers, built by our proposed deCoupled duAl-interactive lineaR attEntion (CARE). 3.3. Asymmetrical Feature Decoupling: Divide and Conquer! Based on the observations in Section 3.2, we propose an asymmetrical feature decoupling strategy. For the input fea- tures X ∈Rhw×d, we first divide them into two parts, rep- resented as ¯X ∈Rhw×d1 and eX ∈Rhw×d2, where the dimensions satisfy d1 +d2 = d. Then, ¯X and eX are fed to a linear attention operation and a local inductive bias learner, ¯X, eX = Split(X, dim=1), (5) ¯X = LinearAtt(¯X),

text: ∈Rhw×d, we first divide them into two parts, rep- resented as ¯X ∈Rhw×d1 and eX ∈Rhw×d2, where the dimensions satisfy d1 +d2 = d. Then, ¯X and eX are fed to a linear attention operation and a local inductive bias learner, ¯X, eX = Split(X, dim=1), (5) ¯X = LinearAtt(¯X), (6) eX = LocalBias( eX), (7) where eX and ¯X indicate the learned features about local inductive bias and long-range dependencies. Thereby, lo- cal and global information can be learned in a divide-and- conquer way. The computational complexity cost in learn- ing local inductive bias and long-range dependencies can be reduced to Ω= 2λ2k2hwd + 4λ1hwd2 + 2λ1hwd2, (8) where λ2 = d2 d and λ1 = ( d1 d )2 are scaling factors about the computational complexity after using our decoupled learn- ing way. ⅁Proposition 3. Linear attention has quadratic complexity to channel dimension, i.e., O(hwd2). Features should be decoupled in an asymmetrical way, meaning d1 + d2 = d and d1 < d2, which further boosts the efficiency of models. ⅁Proof 1. The asymmetrical setting d1 < d2 can reduce computation complexity further. Let d2 −d1 = ∆. There- fore, we have d1 = d−∆ 2 and d2

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: decoupled in an asymmetrical way, meaning d1 + d2 = d and d1 < d2, which further boosts the efficiency of models. ⅁Proof 1. The asymmetrical setting d1 < d2 can reduce computation complexity further. Let d2 −d1 = ∆. There- fore, we have d1 = d−∆ 2 and d2 = d+∆ 2 due to d1 +d2 = d and d2 −d1 = ∆. We can rewrite Equation (8) as follows: Ω(∆) = k2hw(d + ∆) + 3 2hw(d −∆)2. (9) Accordingly, Ω(∆1) −Ω(∆2) = 3 2hw(∆1 −∆2)(2 3k2 + ∆1 + ∆2 −2d) (10) Let ∆1 > 0 and ∆2 = 0, we have Ω(∆1) −Ω(0) < 0 as ∆1 < d and the kernel size generally obeys k2 << d, prov- ing our asymmetrical setting (∆1 > 0) has less complexity compared to the symmetrical scenario (∆2 = 0). 3.4. Decoupled Dual-Interactive Linear Attention Based on the asymmetrical feature decoupling strategy, we propose a new deCoupled duAl-interactive lineaR attEntion (CARE), which is shown in Figure 3 (b). Our CARE fully considers the complementarity of features, and helps the de- coupled learning approach to achieve both high accuracy and efficiency. Notions. For better explanations, in this part, we use

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: feature decoupling strategy, we propose a new deCoupled duAl-interactive lineaR attEntion (CARE), which is shown in Figure 3 (b). Our CARE fully considers the complementarity of features, and helps the de- coupled learning approach to achieve both high accuracy and efficiency. Notions. For better explanations, in this part, we use Xs t ∈ Rhw×d and Zs t ∈Rhw×d′ to represent the features and the dynamic memory unit processed by the t-th CARE block of the s-th stage. Dynamical Memory Unit. Features from different layers have their own advantages and are complementary to each other [3, 36]. Therefore, we introduce a dynamic memory unit in our CARE to dynamically record learned features along the network. For the first CARE block of each stage s, the dynamic memory unit Zs 0 is built by both consider- ing the features and the stored memory from the previous stage (s −1) Zs 0 = CONV2×2(Xs−1 −1 ⊕Zs−1 −1 , stride=2), (11) where Xs−1 −1 and Zs−1 −1 indicate the features and the memory unit updated in the last block of the stage (s −1), and ⊕ denotes a concatenation operation. We set the stride of the 2×2 convolution CONV2×2(·) as 2 to keep the

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: CONV2×2(Xs−1 −1 ⊕Zs−1 −1 , stride=2), (11) where Xs−1 −1 and Zs−1 −1 indicate the features and the memory unit updated in the last block of the stage (s −1), and ⊕ denotes a concatenation operation. We set the stride of the 2×2 convolution CONV2×2(·) as 2 to keep the consistency between the resolutions of the features in the stage (s −1) and s. In this way, the features from previous layers can be replayed and interacted with the features in the subsequent stages. Dual Interaction. CARE considers the dual interaction between features, i.e., i) the interaction between local and global information, and ii) the interaction between features

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: of different layers, which can be described by Equation (12): Xs t, Zs t = Inter2(Inter1(¯Xs t, eXs t), Zs t−1), (12) where ¯Xs t ∈Rhw×d1 and eXs t ∈Rhw×d2 are global and lo- cal features learned in the t-th CARE block of the s-th stage as described in Equation (5), (6), and (7), and Xs t ∈Rhw×d denotes the features that contain both local and global in- formation after the dual interaction. The first interaction function Inter1(·) facilitates the information exchange be- tween local and global features, while the second interac- tion function Inter2(·) further considers the interaction be- tween features in different layers and dynamically updates the memory information Zs t. By conducting the dual inter- action, the information in different types of features learned by the network can be fully considered, which is beneficial for improving accuracy [14, 64]. Dual Interaction Module. As shown in Figure 3 (c), a new dual interaction module is designed to facilitate interaction between features, where the two interaction blocks are uti- lized to implement the two interaction functions Inter1(·) and Inter2(·) respectively. Each interaction block can be described by Inter∗(x, y) = CONV1×1,3×3,1×1(Norm(x ⊕y)), (13) where ∗= 1 or 2 represents

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: (c), a new dual interaction module is designed to facilitate interaction between features, where the two interaction blocks are uti- lized to implement the two interaction functions Inter1(·) and Inter2(·) respectively. Each interaction block can be described by Inter∗(x, y) = CONV1×1,3×3,1×1(Norm(x ⊕y)), (13) where ∗= 1 or 2 represents the index of the interac- tion block and CONV1×1,3×3,1×1(·) denotes a sequence of convolutions. Specifically, we first concatenate x and y to- gether and utilize a normalization layer Norm(·) to normal- ize the inputs. Then, a 1 × 1 convolution is used to perform feature interaction in channel domain and map the inputs to a latent high dimension space with the expansion set as 4. Also, a 3 × 3 depth-wise convolution is adopted to conduct feature interaction in spatial domain. Finally, a 1×1 convo- lution is further utilized to enable feature interaction across channels and map the latent features to the original space. 3.5. CARE Transformers Based on our CARE, we further introduce CARE Trans- formers. As illustrated in Figure 3 (a), our CARE Trans- formers totally contain four stages, each of which is made up of several CARE blocks. We implement the stem layer by using a 4

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: to the original space. 3.5. CARE Transformers Based on our CARE, we further introduce CARE Trans- formers. As illustrated in Figure 3 (a), our CARE Trans- formers totally contain four stages, each of which is made up of several CARE blocks. We implement the stem layer by using a 4 × 4 convolution with the stride set as 4. For CARE-S0, CARE-S1, and CARE-S2, the num- ber of attention blocks in each stage are set as ⟨2, 4, 8, 4⟩, ⟨3, 6, 10, 6⟩, and ⟨3, 6, 10, 6⟩. Accordingly, the dimensions of features are set as ⟨24, 48, 96, 192⟩, ⟨24, 48, 96, 192⟩, and ⟨24, 48, 144, 288⟩. For all of our models, we adopt the asymmetrical setting d1 = 1 2d2, and set the dimension of the dynamic memory unit as d′ = d1. To help models cap- ture local inductive bias in multiscale receptive filed, we im- plement the local bias learner LocalBias(·) using multiple convolutions, i.e., a 3 × 3 and a 7 × 7 depth-wise convolu- tions, utilized in the decoupling manner as well. That means just like Equation (5), (6), and (7), we split local features into two parts, utilize 3 × 3

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: im- plement the local bias learner LocalBias(·) using multiple convolutions, i.e., a 3 × 3 and a 7 × 7 depth-wise convolu- tions, utilized in the decoupling manner as well. That means just like Equation (5), (6), and (7), we split local features into two parts, utilize 3 × 3 and 7 × 7 convolutions to pro- cess them respectively, and then concatenate them together to form the final outputs of LocalBias(·). Also, following [43, 55], linear attention operations are not applied to the first two stages, and a 1 × 11 and a 11 × 1 depth-wise con- volution are employed to aggregate long-range information. 4. Experiments We conduct extensive experiments to demonstrate the effec- tiveness of our method. We first compare our models with counterparts on the classification task of ImageNet-1K [10], and then introduce the results of our models on downstream COCO [28] and ADE20K [62] datasets. Finally, we provide ablation studies to analyze the impact of each component of our models. 4.1. Image Classification Implementation Details. ImageNet-1K [10] is one of the most famous image classification benchmarks, which in- cludes about 1.3 M training images and 50 K test images. On ImageNet-1K, we build our codes

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: Finally, we provide ablation studies to analyze the impact of each component of our models. 4.1. Image Classification Implementation Details. ImageNet-1K [10] is one of the most famous image classification benchmarks, which in- cludes about 1.3 M training images and 50 K test images. On ImageNet-1K, we build our codes by using PyTorch [35] and timm [49], and use AdamW [31] to update model pa- rameters where the initial learning is set as 2e−3. We train our models on 12 RTX 4090 GPU cards for 300 epochs with the batch size set as 128 per GPU. Data augmenta- tion strategies, including standard random resized crop, hor- izontal flip, RandAugment [9], Mixup [58], CutMix [57], random erasing [61], and color jitter are used during train- ing. Also, label smoothing [41], stochastic depth [23], and weight decay are employed to regularize our training pro- cess. Experimental Results. The results on the ImageNet-1K dataset are summarized in Table 1. According to these re- sults, we can draw the following conclusions. Firstly, at the same level of computational costs, our models clearly have higher accuracy. For example, at the cost of 0.7 GMACs, our CARE-S0 can achieve 78.4% accuracy, higher than that of MobileViG-T

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: dataset are summarized in Table 1. According to these re- sults, we can draw the following conclusions. Firstly, at the same level of computational costs, our models clearly have higher accuracy. For example, at the cost of 0.7 GMACs, our CARE-S0 can achieve 78.4% accuracy, higher than that of MobileViG-T and FastViT-T8 by 2.7% and 1.7%. At the cost of 1.9 GMACs, the accuracy of our CARE-S2 is 1.1% and 4.0% higher than that of EdgeViT-S and MobileOne- S3. Secondly, compared with the models having the same level of accuracy, our CARE Transformers have obviously higher efficiency. For example, CARE-S2 just costs half the GMACs of MLLA-T and has 2.5× faster speed on iPhone13 and iPad Pro, yet its accuracy is only lower than that of MLLA-T by 1.4%. These experimental results indi- cate that our models can achieve a better balance between accuracy and efficiency, and demonstrate their superiority in being deployed in resource-constrained mobile devices.

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction

Object Detection & Instance Segmentation Implementation Details. COCO [28] is a frequently-used dataset in the object detection and instance segmentation task, which contains around 118 K training samples and 5 K validation images. On this benchmark, we build our models by using PyTorch [35] and mmdetection [7]. Moreover, we utilize the AdamW [31] optimizer to train our models for 12 epochs where the initial learning rate is set as 2e−4 and the batch size is set as 24. We use the models pre-trained on the ImageNet-1K dataset to initialize the parameters of our backbones. Following the previous works [1, 33], we implement the detection head by using Mask R-CNN [22]. Experimental Results.

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: the initial learning rate is set as 2e−4 and the batch size is set as 24. We use the models pre-trained on the ImageNet-1K dataset to initialize the parameters of our backbones. Following the previous works [1, 33], we implement the detection head by using Mask R-CNN [22]. Experimental Results. The results in Table 2 demon- strate the effectiveness of our method again. On the object detection task, our CARE-S0 outperforms FLatten-PVT- T, SLAB-PVT-T, FastViT-SA12, PoolFormer-S12, and PoolFormer-S24 in both accuracy and efficiency. Although CARE-S1 has obviously less GMCAs, it can still achieve comparable accuracy to MobileViG-M, MobileViGv2-M,

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: model. Similar results can be found on the instance seg- mentation task as well. For instance, our CARE-S2 has obviously higher efficiency than Swin-T, Flatten-Swin-T, Agent-Swin-T, and SLAB-Swin-T, but can still maintain comparable accuracy. These results demonstrate the superi- ority of our method in resource-constrained scenarios. 4.3. Semantic Segmentation Implementation Details. We also evaluate the perfor- mance of our approach on the semantic segmentation task of the ADE20K dataset, which includes about 20 K training samples and 2 K test images from 150 categories. On this dataset, we build our models by using PyTorch [35] and mmsegmentation [8]. We use the checkpoints pre-trained on the ImageNet-1K dataset to initialize our backbone net- works, which are then incorporated with the segmentation head Semantic FPN [25]. We use the AdamW [31] opti- mizer to train our models where the initial learning rate is set as 2e−4 with a polynomial decay schedule and the batch size is set as 48. Experimental Results. The results in Table 2 indicate that our approach can still achieve competitive performance on semantic segmentation. For example, despite that our CARE-S1 just costs half of the GMACs of Agent-PVT- T, FLatten-PVT-T, and PoolFormer-S12, its mIoU is still Table 3.

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: the batch size is set as 48. Experimental Results. The results in Table 2 indicate that our approach can still achieve competitive performance on semantic segmentation. For example, despite that our CARE-S1 just costs half of the GMACs of Agent-PVT- T, FLatten-PVT-T, and PoolFormer-S12, its mIoU is still Table 3. The accuracy of our CARE-S2 is comparable to that of the models Swin-T, FLatten-Swin-T, and Agent-Swin- T. But CARE-S2 has obviously higher efficiency.

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: 24.8 81.4 w/o Local 2.0 20.7 2.5 24.0 77.3 w/ Asym 1.9 19.5 2.0 20.4 82.1 higher than that of these counterparts by 0.83%, 3.8%, and 3.8%. The accuracy of our CARE-S2 is comparable to that of the models Swin-T, FLatten-Swin-T, and Agent-Swin- T. But CARE-S2 has obviously higher efficiency. For ex- ample, the latency of our CARE-S2 is less than that of these three counterparts by 20.9ms, 21.3ms, and 19.5ms on iPhone13, and 0.14s, 0.14s, and 0.12s on the RTX 4090 GPU card. 4.4. Ablation Studies Ablation on the Asymmetrical Feature Decoupling. As can be seen from Table 3, the full asymmetrical decoupling strategy (“w/ Asym”) can help the model to achieve better performance in both efficiency and accuracy. In contrast,

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: the information in learned features, which is better than the alternate use of convolu- tion and linear attention operations. Also, our asymmetrical decoupled learning strategy is still better than the symmetri- cal one (“w/ Sym”), e.g., these two strategies achieve com- parable accuracy but our asymmetrical decoupled learning approach has lower costs in terms of all GAMCs, param- eters, and latency. We also compare our method with the baseline (“w/o Local”) where linear attention is not incorpo- rated with local inductive bias. Without the guidance of lo- cal inductive bias, the accuracy of the baseline is obviously lower than that of ours. Meanwhile, its efficiency is still lower than that of our model, as our asymmetrical feature decoupling can further reduce the quadratic computational overhead of linear attention to channels, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method in solving 7→Q2, i.e., improving the accuracy and efficiency of linear visual Transformers at the same time. Ablation on the Dual Interaction Module. We provide the ablation study for our dual interactive module in Ta- ble 4. According to these results, we have the following observations. The use of the full dual interactive module can achieve the highest top-1 accuracy. While removing the first

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: the same time. Ablation on the Dual Interaction Module. We provide the ablation study for our dual interactive module in Ta- ble 4. According to these results, we have the following observations. The use of the full dual interactive module can achieve the highest top-1 accuracy. While removing the first interaction block (i.e., Inter1) can slightly improve the efficiency of the model, this leads to an obvious 2.2% ac- curacy drop. Discarding the second interaction block (i.e., Inter2) further decreases the accuracy to 70.9%, demon- strating the importance of fully exploiting the complemen- tarity between local inductive bias, long-range dependen- cies, and dynamically stored memory. In Table 4, we also study the influence of the dynamical memory unit. We find removing the dynamical memory unit from Inter2 causes a 1.9% accuracy drop, indicating that exploiting the infor- mation of different layers guides models to achieve better performance. Table 5. Ablation studies for our CARE Transformers. “Early CONV” indicates that long-range information in the first two stages are captured by a 1 × 11 and a 11 × 1 convolution. “ MS” indicates learning local inductive bias in multiscale receptive field. title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: studies for our CARE Transformers. “Early CONV” indicates that long-range information in the first two stages are captured by a 1 × 11 and a 11 × 1 convolution. “ MS” indicates learning local inductive bias in multiscale receptive field. Experiment Backbone Early CONV MS GMACs ↓ Params (M) ↓ Top-1 Acc (%) ↑ CARE-S0 ✓ ✓ 0.72 7.34 78.4 CARE-S0 × ✓ 0.72 7.34 78.3 CARE-S0 ✓ × 0.71 7.32 78.1 CARE-S1 ✓ ✓ 1.03 9.60 80.1 CARE-S1 × ✓ 1.03 9.60 79.9 CARE-S1 ✓ × 1.02 9.57 79.8 CARE-S2 ✓ ✓ 1.93 19.48 82.1 CARE-S2 × ✓ 1.93 19.48 82.0 CARE-S2 ✓ × 1.92 19.43 81.8 Ablation on CARE Transformers. We provide the abla- tion study for our CARE Transformers in Table 5. We find that replacing 1×11 and 11×1 convolutions with linear at- tention in the first two layers causes a slight accuracy drop, as there exists more noisy or redundant information in the shallow layers of neural networks but linear attention’s high entropy property makes it difficult to suppress the influence of noisy or redundant information. The results in the table also demonstrate that learning local inductive bias in multi- scale receptive field can help the

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: more noisy or redundant information in the shallow layers of neural networks but linear attention’s high entropy property makes it difficult to suppress the influence of noisy or redundant information. The results in the table also demonstrate that learning local inductive bias in multi- scale receptive field can help the model achieve better per- formance without sacrificing models’ efficiency obviously. 5. Conclusion In this paper, we propose a new deCoupled duAl-interactive lineaR attEntion (CARE) mechanism, which reveals that features’ decoupling and interaction can fully unleash the power of linear attention. We propose to symmetrically decouple the learning process for local inductive bias and long-range dependencies, thereby preserving sufficient lo- cal and global information while further enhancing the effi- ciency of models. Moreover, we design a dynamic memory unit and a dual interaction module aiming to fully exploit the complementarity between local inductive bias and long- range dependencies as well as among features at different layers. By adopting a decoupled learning way and fully ex- ploiting complementarity across features, our method can achieve both high efficiency and accuracy, showing its su- periority in being deployed in resource-constrained mobile devices. Limitation. There are two limitations in our current work. Firstly, we did

title: CARE Transformer: Mobile-Friendly Linear Visual Transformer via Decoupled Dual Interaction
text: among features at different layers. By adopting a decoupled learning way and fully ex- ploiting complementarity across features, our method can achieve both high efficiency and accuracy, showing its su- periority in being deployed in resource-constrained mobile devices. Limitation. There are two limitations in our current work. Firstly, we did not utilize NAS to search for the optimal architecture of our CARE Transformers. Secondly, we did not apply our method to models with relatively larger size due to our limited GPU resources; however, we believe that the proposed CARE mechanism can also perform well with large models. We will study these two points in the future.
